record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk (Oregon county clerks handle recording of deeds, unlike many states that have a separate County Recorder)
record_office_terminology.probate: County Circuit Court (probate jurisdiction in Oregon courts)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Oregon required no waiting period historically.
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1903 (statewide registration). County clerks and churches recorded some vital events earlier. Birth and death records before 1903 are fragmentary and scattered among county clerks, churches, and the Oregon State Archives.

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Oregon Record Losses; Oregon State Archives guides. Oregon has no burned counties, but some early territorial records are sparse, particularly in remote eastern Oregon counties.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Oregon has no burned counties. Record survival is generally excellent, especially in the Willamette Valley counties with records from the Provisional Government era (1843). The Heppner Flood (1903) destroyed private records but courthouse records survived. Some early territorial records in remote eastern Oregon counties are sparse due to small populations rather than losses.

administrative_notes: Oregon's governmental history is uniquely layered: Oregon Country was jointly occupied by the US and Britain until 1846; the Provisional Government (1843–1848) created the first American governmental records west of the Rockies; Oregon Territory was organized in 1848; statehood came in 1859 as the 33rd state. The four original Provisional Government districts — Clackamas, Champoeg (later Marion), Tuality (later Washington), and Yamhill — have records from 1843. Wasco County, formed in 1854, once encompassed all of eastern Oregon (the largest county in US history). The Oregon State Archives in Salem is the central state repository. County clerks handle both recording (deeds) and election functions. The Donation Land Claim Act (1850–1855) is a key genealogical source unique to Oregon — it granted free land to settlers and required detailed biographical information.

core_repositories.description: Central state repository holding Provisional Government records (1843–1848), territorial government records (1848–1859), state agency records, transferred county records, vital records, military records, and legislative archives. The primary repository for Oregon genealogical research.

Premier Oregon history repository with manuscript collections, photograph archives, maps, family files, Oregon Trail emigrant diaries, fur trade records, newspaper indexes, and the Oregon Historical Quarterly. Research library is essential for Oregon genealogy.

core_repositories.source_name: University of Oregon — Special Collections and University Archives
core_repositories.description: Major research collection for Oregon and Pacific Northwest history including logging industry records, Oregon Country manuscripts, political papers, and oral histories.

core_repositories.source_name: Oregon State University — Special Collections and Archives Research Center
core_repositories.description: Agricultural history, forestry records, Willamette Valley manuscripts, and Oregon Extension Service records relevant to rural communities.

Federal records for Oregon including BLM land patent records, Chinese Exclusion Act case files, BIA records for Oregon tribal communities, federal court records (including naturalization), and military installation records.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive microfilmed and digitized Oregon county records including marriage records, probate files, land records, and vital records from the territorial era onward. Coverage varies by county but Willamette Valley counties have records from the 1840s.
Indexed death records from Oregon state registration. Searchable by name, date, and county.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Oregon newspapers including The Oregonian (Portland, 1850), Oregon Spectator (Oregon City, 1846 — first newspaper west of the Rockies), East Oregonian (Pendleton), and dozens of other territorial and statehood-era papers.
Oregon-specific digital newspaper project providing access to historic Oregon newspapers beyond what Chronicling America covers. Includes many smaller community papers.

military_records.description: Oregon militia records, Indian War volunteer muster rolls (Cayuse War, Rogue River Wars, Modoc War), Civil War enlistments, National Guard records, and WWI/WWII service records. Oregon Adjutant General records.

Federal military records from Oregon installations including Fort Vancouver, Fort Dalles, Fort Klamath, Fort Yamhill, Fort Stevens, Camp Adair, and Camp White. Personnel records, post returns, and military campaign records.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Oregon
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management / GLO Records
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Oregon including Donation Land Claims (1850–1855), homestead entries, timber culture entries, and mineral claims. Donation Land Claims are uniquely valuable — they contain biographical data, marriage information, and immigration histories of Oregon Trail settlers. Searchable online database.

Oregon's 36 county clerks maintain deed records, mortgages, liens, and related land instruments. In Oregon, the county clerk serves as both the recorder and elections official (unlike states with separate recorder offices). Online access varies widely — urban counties have better online systems; rural counties generally require in-person visits.

vital_records.source_name: Oregon Health Authority — Center for Health Statistics (Vital Records)
vital_records.description: State vital records from 1903 (statewide registration). Birth and death certificates available to qualifying applicants. Marriage records indexed from 1906. Pre-1903 records are scattered among county clerks and churches.

County clerks have issued marriage licenses since the Provisional Government era. Some Willamette Valley counties have marriage records from the 1840s. Marriage records before 1906 are exclusively at the county level.

church_and_cemetery.description: Cemetery records and burial transcriptions for Oregon cemeteries including pioneer cemeteries from the 1840s, mining camp burial grounds, and municipal cemeteries across all 36 counties.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Oregon Commission on Historic Cemeteries / DAR chapters
church_and_cemetery.description: Pioneer cemetery transcriptions compiled by the Oregon Commission on Historic Cemeteries and DAR chapters. Covers many of Oregon's earliest burial grounds dating to the 1840s–1860s.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Oregon's largest genealogical society with a research library in Portland. Publishes The Bulletin (quarterly) and maintains indexes, databases, and Oregon-specific research guides.
Comprehensive research guide for Oregon genealogy including county-by-county record availability, Donation Land Claim research tips, record loss information, and links to online resources.
State library with genealogical resources including Oregon newspapers on microfilm, state document collections, and inter-library loan access to genealogical materials.

historical_counties_extinct.notes: One of the four original Provisional Government districts (1843). Renamed Marion County in 1849. Champoeg was the site of the 1843 vote to form the Provisional Government. Records at Oregon State Archives.
historical_counties_extinct.records_location: Oregon State Archives, Salem; Marion County Clerk, Salem

historical_counties_extinct.notes: One of the four original Provisional Government districts (1843). Renamed Washington County in 1849. Named for the Tualatin (Atfalati) Kalapuya people.
historical_counties_extinct.records_location: Oregon State Archives, Salem; Washington County Records Division, Hillsboro

historical_counties_extinct.notes: Formed in 1851, absorbed by Douglas County in 1862. Named for the Umpqua River and people. Records transferred to Douglas County.
historical_counties_extinct.records_location: Douglas County Clerk, Roseburg; Oregon State Archives, Salem

historical_counties_extinct.notes: Alternate early spelling of Tuality. The original 1843 district used this spelling briefly before standardizing on 'Tuality.'

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Bureau of Indian Affairs — Oregon Superintendency Records
native_american_and_removal_records.description: BIA records for Oregon tribal communities including the Oregon Superintendency records (1848–1880), agency records for Siletz, Grand Ronde, Warm Springs, Umatilla, and Klamath agencies. Treaty records, census rolls, allotment records, termination files, and restoration documentation.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Oregon Indian Tribal Termination and Restoration Records
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records from the federal termination policy that dissolved Oregon tribal governments (Klamath 1954, Siletz 1954, Grand Ronde 1954, and others) and subsequent restoration acts (1977–1989). Termination rolls are detailed member lists invaluable for genealogy.

Student enrollment records, academic files, and photographs from Chemawa Indian School in Salem — the oldest continuously operating Indian boarding school in the US. Documents Native American students from across the Pacific Northwest.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Chinese Exclusion Act Case Files — Oregon
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Case files from enforcement of the Chinese Exclusion Act in Oregon (1882–1943). Documents Chinese miners, railroad workers, merchants, and laborers in Portland, Baker County, Grant County, and other communities. Includes photographs, affidavits, and testimony.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Japanese American Community and WWII Internment Records — Oregon
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Washington / Oregon Nikkei Endowment / Oregon Historical Society
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Records of Japanese farming communities in Hood River, Malheur County, and Portland. War Relocation Authority records for Oregon Japanese Americans. Malheur County uniquely accepted Japanese Americans during WWII as an alternative to internment.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Oregon Naturalization Records — Federal and County Courts
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NARA Seattle / Oregon State Archives / Individual county courts
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from Oregon federal and county courts. Documents Scandinavian loggers, Chinese laborers, Japanese farmers, Finnish fishermen, Swiss dairy farmers, and other immigrant communities across Oregon.
